khan_exercises.title: Amplitude of sinusoidal functions from graph
khan_exercises.description: Given the graph of a sinusoidal function, determine its amplitude.
topic_path: Amplitude of sinusoidal functions from graph

items.question_content:
Below is the graph of a trigonometric function. It has a maximum point at $\left(-5,2.5\right)$ and a minimum point at $\left(-1.7,-4.5\right)$.

[[☃ image 2]]

**What is the amplitude of the function?**  

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] units
hints:
###Defining amplitude

The amplitude of a sinusoidal function is *equal to half the vertical distance from its minimum to its maximum.*


$\text{amplitude}=\dfrac{\text{max value}-\text{min value}}{2}$

Since the *midline* of a sinusoidal function passes exactly in the middle of its extreme values, the amplitude is also *equal to the vertical distance from its midline to one of its extrema.*

$\text{amplitude}=\text{max value}-\text{midline value}$

$\text{amplitude}=\text{midline value}-\text{min value}$

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/02e27bb913f63bba924a054740716e880ada1f2e)

### Thinking about the problem

We are given the minimum and maximum values of the function. Therefore, we should use the *first* formula in order to find the amplitude.

###The answer

The amplitude of the function is $3.5$ units.

options.alt: A graph of a trigonometric wave on an x y coordinate plane. The x and y axes scale by one. There is a point on the highest part of the graph labeled (negative five, two and one half). There is a point on the lowest part of the graph labeled (negative one and seven tenths, negative four and one half).

answers.message: This is the distance between the maximum and the minimum, but the amplitude is equal to half this distance.

items.question_content:
Below is the graph of a trigonometric function. It has a maximum point at $\left(1.5,3.2\right)$ and a minimum point at $\left(4.75,-1.6\right)$.

[[☃ image 2]]

**What is the amplitude of the function?**  

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] units
hints:
###Defining amplitude

The amplitude of a sinusoidal function is *equal to half the vertical distance from its minimum to its maximum.*


$\text{amplitude}=\dfrac{\text{max value}-\text{min value}}{2}$

Since the *midline* of a sinusoidal function passes exactly in the middle of its extreme values, the amplitude is also *equal to the vertical distance from its midline to one of its extrema.*

$\text{amplitude}=\text{max value}-\text{midline value}$

$\text{amplitude}=\text{midline value}-\text{min value}$

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/02e27bb913f63bba924a054740716e880ada1f2e)

### Thinking about the problem

We are given the minimum and maximum values of the function. Therefore, we should use the *first* formula in order to find the amplitude.
###Calculating amplitude

According to the graph, the maximum value of the function is $3.2$ and its minimum value is $-1.6$.

$\begin{align}\text{amplitude}&=\dfrac{\text{max value}-\text{min value}}{2}
\\\\
&=\dfrac{3.2-(-1.6)}{2}
\\\\
&=\dfrac{4.8}{2}
\\\\
&=2.4
\end{align}$



###The answer

The amplitude of the function is $2.4$ units.

options.alt: A graph of a trigonometric wave on an x y coordinate plane. The x and y axes scale by one. There is a point on the highest part of the graph labeled (one and one half, three and two tenths). There is a point on the lowest part of the graph labeled (four and seventy five hundredths, negative one and six tenths).

answers.message: This is the distance between the maximum and the minimum, but the amplitude is equal to half this distance.

###Defining amplitude

The amplitude of a sinusoidal function is *equal to half the vertical distance from its minimum to its maximum.*

items.question_content:
Below is the graph of a trigonometric function. It has a maximum point at $\left(-5,2.5\right)$ and a minimum point at $\left(-1.7,-4.5\right)$.

![A graph of a trigonometric wave on an x y coordinate plane. The x and y axes scale by one. There is a maximum point of the graph at (negative 5, two point 5). There is a minimum point of the graph at (negative one point seven, negative four point five).](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/165be9596bccc8080cf0dc01a79af3c7d253f853)

**What is the period of the function? Give an exact value.**

[[☃ expression 2]] units
hints:
###Defining period

The period of a sinusoidal function is the horizontal distance between the ends of a single cycle of its graph.

![A trigonometric wave that goes up from left to right then down, then back up again. There is a horizontal dashed line in the middle of the wave labeled the midline. A vertical dashed line from the midline to the highest part of the wave is labeled the amplitude. Another vertical dashed line from the midline to the lowest part of the wave is labeled the amplitude as well. The wave starts with a point at the midline, curves up to a highest point, curves back down through a point on the midline to a lowest point. It then curves back up to a point on the midline. From its initial point on the midline to the last point on the midline is labeled one period. The horizontal distance from the initial point on the midline to the highest point is labeled one forth the period. The horizontal distance from the highest point to the next point on the midline is labeled one forth the period. The horizontal distance from the second point on the midline to the lowest point is labeled one forth the period. The horizontal distance from the lowest point to the last point on the midline is labeled one forth the period.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/5c7f57ce4419be9d9d62ba248bf6b2ec8d55a90b)

The horizontal distance between an extreme value and a neighboring intersection point with the midline is always equal to a quarter period.   [[☃ explanation 1]]

### Thinking about the problem

In this case, we are given the maximum and minimum points on the graph of the function.  Therefore, if we find the horizontal distance between these two points, it should be equal to *half* the period.

###Calculating the period

We know that the function has a maximum value of $2.5$ at $x=-5$ and has its minimum value of $-4.5$ at $x=-1.7$.

So, the horizontal distance between the maximum and minimum points is:

$-1.7-(-5)=3.3$

Since this is *half* of the period, we can multiply by $2$ to find our answer. 

$2\cdot3.3=6.6$

###The answer

The period of the function is $6.6$ units.

$4.7-(-4.7)=9.4$

Since this is a *quarter* of the period, we can multiply by $4$ to find our answer. 

$4\cdot9.4=37.6$

###The answer

The horizontal distance between an extreme value and a neighboring intersection point with the midline is always equal to a quarter period.  [[☃ explanation 1]]

### Thinking about the problem

In this case, we are given a maximum point and a neighboring intersection point with the midline.  Therefore, if we find the horizontal distance between these two points, it should be equal to a *quarter* of the period.

###Calculating the period

We know that the function has a maximum point at $\left(4.1,0.7\right)$ and a neighboring intersection point with the midline of $\left(-3.5,-0.8\right)$. 

So, the horizontal distance between these points is:

$4.1-(-3.5)=7.6$

Since this is a *quarter* of the period, we can multiply by $4$ to find our answer. 

$4\cdot7.6=30.4$

###The answer

The period of the function is $30.4$ units.

items.question_content:
Below is the graph of a trigonometric function. It has a maximum point at $\left(-5,2.5\right)$ and a minimum point at $\left(-1.7,-4.5\right)$.


[[☃ image 1]]

**What is the midline equation of the function? **

$y=$ [[☃ numeric-input 2]]
hints:
###Defining midline

The midline of a sinusoidal function passes *exactly in the middle of its extreme values*. So we can find the midline by finding the *average* of the maximum and minimum values.  

$\text{midline}=\dfrac{\text{max value}+\text{min value}}{2}$

Since the amplitude of a sinusoid is equal to the vertical distance from its midline to one of its extrema, we can also calculate the midline using one of the following formulas.

$\text{midline}=\text{max value}-\text{amplitude}$ 
 
$\text{midline}=\text{min value}+\text{amplitude}$


![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/02e27bb913f63bba924a054740716e880ada1f2e)

### Thinking about the problem

We are given the minimum and maximum values of the function. Therefore, we should use the *first* formula in order to find the midline.
###Calculating the midline

According to the graph, the maximum value of the function is $2.5$, and its minimum value is $-4.5$.

$\begin{align}\text{midline}&=\dfrac{\text{max value}+\text{min value}}{2}\\\\
&=\dfrac{2.5+(-4.5)}{2}
\\\\
&=\dfrac{-2}{2}
\\\\
&=-1
\end{align}$

###The answer

The midline of the function is $y=-1$.

There is a point on the lowest part of the graph labeled (negative one and seven tenths, negative four and a half).

answers.message: This is the amplitude of the function, not the equation for its midline.

Below is the graph of a trigonometric function. It has a minimum point at $\left(\dfrac{2\pi}{3},-8.2\right)$ and an amplitude of $5.4$.

[[☃ image 1]]

**What is the midline equation of the function? **

$y=$ [[☃ numeric-input 2]]
hints:
###Defining midline

The midline of a sinusoidal function passes *exactly in the middle of its extreme values*. So we can find the midline by finding the average of the maximum and minimum values.  

$\text{midline}=\dfrac{\text{max value}+\text{min value}}{2}$

Since the amplitude of a sinusoid is equal to the vertical distance from its midline to one of its extrema, we can also calculate the midline using one of the following formulas.

$\text{midline}=\text{max value}-\text{amplitude}$ 
 
$\text{midline}=\text{min value}+\text{amplitude}$

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/02e27bb913f63bba924a054740716e880ada1f2e)

### Thinking about the problem

We are given the minimum value and the amplitude of the function. Therefore, we should use the *third* formula in order to find the midline.
###Calculating the midline

According to the graph, the minimum value of the function is $-8.2$, and the amplitude is $5.4$.

$\begin{align}\text{midline}&=\text{min value}+\text{amplitude}
\\\\
&=-8.2+5.4
\\\\
&=-2.8
\end{align}$

###The answer

The midline of the function is $y=-2.8$.

items.question_content:
Below is the graph of a trigonometric function. It has a maximum point at $\left(-5,2.5\right)$ and a minimum point at $\left(-1.7,-4.5\right)$.

[[☃ image 2]]

**What is the amplitude of the function?**  

[[☃ numeric-input 1]] units
hints:
###Defining amplitude

The amplitude of a sinusoidal function is *equal to half the vertical distance from its minimum to its maximum.*


$\text{amplitude}=\dfrac{\text{max value}-\text{min value}}{2}$

Since the *midline* of a sinusoidal function passes exactly in the middle of its extreme values, the amplitude is also *equal to the vertical distance from its midline to one of its extrema.*

$\text{amplitude}=\text{max value}-\text{midline value}$

$\text{amplitude}=\text{midline value}-\text{min value}$

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/02e27bb913f63bba924a054740716e880ada1f2e)

### Thinking about the problem

We are given the minimum and maximum values of the function. Therefore, we should use the *first* formula in order to find the amplitude.

###The answer

The amplitude of the function is $3.5$ units.

There is a point on the lowest part of the graph labeled (negative one and seven tenths, negative four and one half).

answers.message: This is the distance between the maximum and the minimum, but the amplitude is equal to half this distance.

###Defining amplitude

The amplitude of a sinusoidal function is *equal to half the vertical distance from its minimum to its maximum.*


$\text{amplitude}=\dfrac{\text{max value}-\text{min value}}{2}$

Since the *midline* of a sinusoidal function passes exactly in the middle of its extreme values, the amplitude is also *equal to the vertical distance from its midline to one of its extrema.*

$\text{amplitude}=\dfrac{\text{max value}-\text{min value}}{2}$

Since the *midline* of a sinusoidal function passes exactly in the middle of its extreme values, the amplitude is also *equal to the vertical distance from its midline to one of its extrema.*

$\text{amplitude}=\text{max value}-\text{midline value}$

$\text{amplitude}=\text{midline value}-\text{min value}$

$\begin{align}\text{amplitude}&={\text{max value}-\text{midline value}}
\\\\
&=-2.7-(-10.1)
\\\\
&=7.4
\end{align}$


###The answer

The amplitude of the function is $7.4$ units.

Below is the graph of a trigonometric function. It has a maximum point at $\left(-5,2.5\right)$ and a minimum point at $\left(-1.7,-4.5\right)$.


[[☃ image 1]]

**What is the midline equation of the function? **

$y=$ [[☃ numeric-input 2]]
hints:
###Defining midline

The midline of a sinusoidal function passes *exactly in the middle of its extreme values*. So we can find the midline by finding the *average* of the maximum and minimum values.  

$\text{midline}=\dfrac{\text{max value}+\text{min value}}{2}$

Since the amplitude of a sinusoid is equal to the vertical distance from its midline to one of its extrema, we can also calculate the midline using one of the following formulas.

$\text{midline}=\text{max value}-\text{amplitude}$ 
 
$\text{midline}=\text{min value}+\text{amplitude}$


![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/02e27bb913f63bba924a054740716e880ada1f2e)

### Thinking about the problem

We are given the minimum and maximum values of the function. Therefore, we should use the *first* formula in order to find the midline.
###Calculating the midline

According to the graph, the maximum value of the function is $2.5$, and its minimum value is $-4.5$.

$\begin{align}\text{midline}&=\dfrac{\text{max value}+\text{min value}}{2}\\\\
&=\dfrac{2.5+(-4.5)}{2}
\\\\
&=\dfrac{-2}{2}
\\\\
&=-1
\end{align}$

###The answer

The midline of the function is $y=-1$.

items.question_content:
Below is the graph of a trigonometric function. It has a minimum point at $\left(-\dfrac{1}{2}\pi,2.4\right)$ and a maximum point at $\left(\dfrac{3}{2}\pi,13\right)$.


[[☃ image 1]]

**What is the midline equation of the function? Give an exact expression.**

$y=$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
###Defining midline

The midline of a sinusoidal function passes *exactly in the middle of its extreme values*. So we can find the midline by finding the *average* of the maximum and minimum values.  

$\text{midline}=\dfrac{\text{max value}+\text{min value}}{2}$

Since the amplitude of a sinusoid is equal to the vertical distance from its midline to one of its extrema, we can also calculate the midline using one of the following formulas.

$\text{midline}=\text{max value}-\text{amplitude}$ 
 
$\text{midline}=\text{min value}+\text{amplitude}$


![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/02e27bb913f63bba924a054740716e880ada1f2e)

### Thinking about the problem

We are given the minimum and maximum values of the function. Therefore, we should use the *first* formula in order to find the midline.

###The answer

The midline equation of the function is $y=7.7$.

items.question_content:
Below is the graph of a trigonometric function. It has a maximum point at $\left(-3,4\right)$ and an amplitude of $4.6$.



[[☃ image 1]]

**What is the midline equation of the function? **

$y=$ [[☃ numeric-input 2]]
hints:
###Defining midline

The midline of a sinusoidal function passes *exactly in the middle of its extreme values*. So we can find the midline by finding the average of the maximum and minimum values.  

$\text{midline}=\dfrac{\text{max value}+\text{min value}}{2}$

Since the amplitude of a sinusoid is equal to the vertical distance from its midline to one of its extrema, we can also calculate the midline using one of the following formulas.

$\text{midline}=\text{max value}-\text{amplitude}$ 
 
$\text{midline}=\text{min value}+\text{amplitude}$

![](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/02e27bb913f63bba924a054740716e880ada1f2e)

### Thinking about the problem

We are given the maximum value and the amplitude of the function. Therefore, we should use the *second* formula in order to find the midline.
###Calculating the midline

According to the graph, the maximum value of the function is $4$, and the amplitude is $4.6$.

$\begin{align}\text{midline}&=\text{max value}-\text{amplitude}
\\\\
&=4-4.6
\\\\
&=-0.6
\end{align}$

###The answer

The midline of the function is $y=-0.6$.
